item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm iffy on the use of old articles in such a meta fashion, but I just don't care for "things that affect Foundation personnel." I feel like anomalies that only target one organization or sub-group or whatever need to be particularly interesting to make up for the immersion breaking-ness of "this anomalous thing only exists in relation to this one group of people." This didn't do it, so -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Personally, the piece I truly liked was how the first report described an object falsely rendered docile by feeding it an experimental compound, and ended with an object that was supposedly actually rendered docile by feeding it an experimental compound. The back and forth is quite enjoyable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm really, really liking the implication that modern articles aren't grimdark enough and we're all going to die because of it. I'd +1 again if I could.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
PS in my headcanon the original procedures are (mostly) correct/appropriate and this skip is trying to cause containment breaches by making us think things have been added that shouldn't.

You know, I think you just got me to rescind a downvote of the "Like, we all know Pandora's Box was dumb." variety mentioned below. I'm a big fan of Re:The Statue-type interpretations where the Foundation does in fact know what it's doing most of the time, and there are reasons behind a lot of those things.

Still not an upvote, but it does temper the "yes, we are aware that old articles can be really grimdark and/or have weird plot holes" gut reaction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, you do have a point, though. My intention with this one was the exact opposite: I wanted people to realize old articles are simple, but we can interpret them in a lot of different ways today. Heck, what inspired me to write this was exactly some mean-spirited comments I've read towards 076.

I made a few additions in attempt to prevent this from being read as derogatory. Thanks for pointing that out!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My first thought was… if you want to call a bunch of popular articles dumb, you can just say "I think they're dumb", you don't have to write an article about it.

But I'm probably being too cynical/projecting. I get the commentary of "look how much things have changed in 10 years of writing". Something about the wording still feels a bit smug though. Especially calling 110-Montauk "nonsensical torture". Sure, I guess it is if you assume that Scranton Reality Anchors can solve all our problems. But that's not less nonsensical. Same with the implication that Pandora's Box was super dumb and the Foundation would never do it. Like, we all know Pandora's Box was dumb. There's a note on the article that specifically says "Pandora's Box was dumb".

Sorry, like I said, projecting. I'm sure this was meant to be "re-contextualising classic stories" and not "shitting on stuff I don't like" lol. And some of the ideas (especially the idea of advanced technology making unethical/dangerous containment procedures obsolete) were really interesting. If it was more "we should use remote drones to clean 173" and less "cross-testing SCPs to kill 682 is dumb" I would be way more into it.

EDIT: Just saw your comment above and realised that my suggestion would not really be conducive to what you're aiming for with this article so no worries. Would still make for a dope tale though I think. Seeing how the Foundation updates their containment procedures over time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Spoilers!
- Hide Spoilers

You're correct! I just added a few more details to make the connection a tad more obvious.

The purpose here was two-fold: First, I wanted to create a feeling of paranoia. Were things added? Is this document itself reliable?

The second thing was that I've seen a lot of complaints about older articles in the recent posts lately. This was an attempt to explain the shift in the site's writing standards from an in-universe point of view.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was neutral on this at first until I read it closely enough to see that each of those log entries corresponds to a classic SCP (or maybe I just interpreted it that way). Skips that primarily affect the documentation of other skips are familiar territory by now, but this one does it really cleverly. Definite +1.

PS in my headcanon the original procedures are (mostly) correct/appropriate and this skip is trying to cause containment breaches by making us think things have been added that shouldn't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, I understand the feeling. There is somewhat of a curve ball in the logs - but I think I might have stuck a point where it's too subtle to be noticed or even make a difference :|

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a solid foundation, but it feels sort of lacking. Like, there's a lot of cool directions you could go with this, but on its own it isn't super interesting. The logs added almost nothing, they just confirmed that, yup, it makes people misread containment procedures in destructive ways.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoting for the novelty of seeing foundation staff deal with compromised documentation. We as readers see that all the time in skips like 2343 or 2316 but getting to see a reaction to the phenomenon from in universe personnel was interesting.
